1:
"And I heard a great voice out of the temple saying to the seven angels, Go your ways, and pour out the vials of the wrath of God upon the earth." The witnesses note the command to pour out the vials of wrath. On "I heard a great voice out of the temple saying to the seven angels," the grammatical voices note the command from God's presence. Barnes and Robertson note the directive: "a great voice out of the temple saying to the seven angels, Go your ways, and pour out the vials of the wrath of God upon the earth" — a great voice from the temple (from God, 15:8) commands the seven angels to pour out their bowls of wrath; the order is given for the final judgments to begin.

On "Go your ways, and pour out the vials of the wrath of God," the witnesses note the outpouring. Barnes and Ellicott note that the angels are bidden to "pour out the vials (bowls) of the wrath of God upon the earth" — the seven last plagues (ch. 15) are now to be emptied on the earth; the command initiates the series of vial-judgments.

On the source, the latin-fathers Bede and the grammatical-historical note the temple: the voice comes "out of the temple" — the command for the judgments proceeds from God's sanctuary (15:5-8); the vials of wrath are poured out by divine authority; the judgments are God's own appointed acts. The verse displays the command to pour out the vials of wrath.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the command — "a great voice out of the temple saying to the seven angels, Go your ways, and pour out the vials of the wrath of God upon the earth," the divine order for the final judgments to begin (15:8, Barnes, Robertson); "pour out the vials of the wrath of God," the seven last plagues emptied on the earth (Barnes, Ellicott); the voice "out of the temple," the judgments by divine authority (Bede, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vial-command. The verse marks the voice: from the temple. The verse displays the command: pour out the vials. The verse displays the wrath: of God. The verse displays the target: the earth. The command displays the final judgments ordered from God's sanctuary. The verse displays the voice: great. The verse displays the angels: commanded. The verse displays the vials: poured. The command to pour out the vials of wrath displays the opening of the final judgments: a great voice from the temple bids the seven angels go and pour out the vials of God's wrath upon the earth — the seven last plagues, ordered by divine authority. The verse displays the vial-command, the first poured on the earth (v.2).
2:
"And the first went, and poured out his vial upon the earth; and there fell a noisome and grievous sore upon the men which had the mark of the beast, and upon them which worshipped his image." The witnesses note the first vial bringing a grievous sore on the beast-worshippers. On "the first went, and poured out his vial upon the earth," the grammatical voices note the first plague. Barnes and Robertson note the outpouring: "the first went, and poured out his vial upon the earth; and there fell a noisome (evil, foul) and grievous (painful) sore" — the first angel pours his bowl on the earth, and a foul, painful sore breaks out; the plague recalls the boils of Egypt (Exodus 9:9-11); the vials parallel the Egyptian plagues, as judgments on a new Egypt/Babylon.

On "a noisome and grievous sore," the witnesses note the affliction. Barnes and Gill note that the "noisome and grievous sore" is a painful, loathsome ulcer or affliction; whether literal or (in the historicist/symbolic reading) a moral and spiritual plague — a corrupting, tormenting judgment on the followers of the beast.

On "upon the men which had the mark of the beast, and upon them which worshipped his image," the grammatical-historical note the targeted judgment: the sore fell "upon the men which had the mark of the beast, and... worshipped his image" — the plague is directed specifically at the beast-worshippers (ch. 13); those who took the mark and worshiped the image are the objects of the first vial; the judgment falls on the adherents of the antichristian power. The verse displays the first vial bringing a grievous sore on the beast-worshippers.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the plague — "the first... poured out his vial upon the earth; and there fell a noisome and grievous sore," the first bowl bringing a foul, painful sore, recalling Egypt's boils (Exodus 9, Barnes, Robertson); the sore a loathsome affliction, moral/spiritual to symbolists (Barnes, Gill); "upon the men which had the mark of the beast, and... worshipped his image," the judgment targeted at the beast-worshippers (ch. 13, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the first plague. The verse marks the vial: on the earth. The verse displays the sore: noisome and grievous. The verse displays the target: the marked. The verse displays the image-worshippers: afflicted. The plague displays the beast's followers struck with a grievous sore. The verse displays the vial: poured. The verse displays the sore: painful. The verse displays the marked: smitten. The first vial bringing a grievous sore on the beast-worshippers displays the opening plague: the first angel pours his bowl on the earth, and a foul, painful sore falls on those who bore the mark of the beast and worshiped its image — the judgment targeted at the antichristian power's followers. The verse displays the first plague, the second poured on the sea (v.3).
3:
"And the second angel poured out his vial upon the sea; and it became as the blood of a dead man: and every living soul died in the sea." The brief verse notes the second vial turning the sea to blood. The grammatical voices note the plague on the sea. Barnes and Robertson note the outpouring: "the second angel poured out his vial upon the sea; and it became as the blood of a dead man: and every living soul died in the sea" — the second angel pours his bowl on the sea, which becomes like the blood of a corpse (dark, clotted, putrid), and every living thing in the sea dies; the plague recalls the first Egyptian plague (Exodus 7:20-21, the Nile turned to blood) and the second trumpet (8:8-9).

On "it became as the blood of a dead man," the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Ellicott note that the sea became "as the blood of a dead man" — not fresh blood, but the dark, corrupt blood of a corpse; the figure of utter corruption and death; whether literal or symbolic of a judgment corrupting and destroying in the realm signified by the sea (the nations).

On "every living soul died in the sea," the grammatical-historical note the total destruction: "every living soul (every living thing) died in the sea" — all sea-life perishes; the plague brings complete death to the sea; the figure of a sweeping, deadly judgment; the second vial extends the wrath from the land to the sea. The verse displays the second vial turning the sea to blood.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the plague — "the second angel poured out his vial upon the sea; and it became as the blood of a dead man," the sea turned to corpse-blood (Exodus 7:20; 8:8-9, Barnes, Robertson); "as the blood of a dead man" the dark, corrupt blood of death, literal or symbolic (Barnes, Ellicott); "every living soul died in the sea," complete death to the sea, a sweeping judgment (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bloodied sea. The verse marks the vial: on the sea. The verse displays the blood: of a dead man. The verse displays the death: every living soul. The verse displays the parallel: Egypt's plague. The plague displays the sea corrupted and slain. The verse displays the sea: bloodied. The verse displays the blood: corpse-like. The verse displays the life: dead. The second vial turning the sea to blood displays the second plague: the second angel pours his bowl on the sea, which becomes like the blood of a dead man, and every living thing in it dies — the sweeping, deadly judgment extending the wrath to the sea, recalling Egypt's plague. The verse displays the bloodied sea, the third poured on the rivers (v.4).
4:
"And the third angel poured out his vial upon the rivers and fountains of waters; and they became blood." The brief verse notes the third vial turning the rivers to blood. The grammatical voices note the plague on the fresh waters. Barnes and Robertson note the outpouring: "the third angel poured out his vial upon the rivers and fountains of waters; and they became blood" — the third angel pours his bowl on the rivers and springs, which become blood; the plague extends the bloody judgment from the sea (v.3) to the fresh waters; again recalling the Egyptian plague (Exodus 7:19-20) and the third trumpet (8:10-11).

On "upon the rivers and fountains of waters," the witnesses note the scope. Barnes and Ellicott note that the plague falls on "the rivers and fountains of waters" — the sources of fresh water and drink; the judgment now strikes the very springs and rivers; whether literal or symbolic of a corrupting judgment on the sources of life/sustenance in the realm of the beast.

On "they became blood," the grammatical-historical note the bloody waters: "they became blood" — the rivers and springs turn to blood, fitting the retribution declared in vv.5-6 (blood for those who shed the blood of the saints); the waters of drink become blood, a judgment answering the bloodshed of the persecutors. The verse displays the third vial turning the rivers to blood.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the plague — "the third angel poured out his vial upon the rivers and fountains of waters; and they became blood," the rivers and springs turned to blood (Exodus 7:19; 8:10-11, Barnes, Robertson); "the rivers and fountains of waters" the sources of fresh water, literal or symbolic (Barnes, Ellicott); "they became blood," the bloody waters answering the persecutors' bloodshed (vv.5-6, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the bloodied rivers. The verse marks the vial: on the rivers. The verse displays the fountains: of waters. The verse displays the result: blood. The verse displays the retribution: blood for blood. The plague displays the fresh waters turned to blood. The verse displays the rivers: bloodied. The verse displays the springs: of blood. The verse displays the judgment: fitting. The third vial turning the rivers to blood displays the third plague: the third angel pours his bowl on the rivers and fountains, which become blood — the bloody judgment extended to the fresh waters, a fitting retribution for the blood of the saints. The verse displays the bloodied rivers, vindicated by the angel of the waters (vv.5-6).
5:
"And I heard the angel of the waters say, Thou art righteous, O Lord, which art, and wast, and shalt be, because thou hast judged thus." The brief verse notes the angel of the waters vindicating God's righteous judgment. The grammatical voices note the vindication. Barnes and Robertson note the angel's praise: "I heard the angel of the waters say, Thou art righteous, O Lord, which art, and wast, and shalt be, because thou hast judged thus" — the angel set over the waters (the agent or guardian of that element) declares God righteous in this judgment; the eternal God (who is, was, and shall be) is justified in turning the waters to blood.

On "Thou art righteous, O Lord, which art, and wast, and shalt be," the witnesses note the address. Barnes and Ellicott note that the angel addresses the eternal God (the title from 1:4, 8, here "which art, and wast, and shalt be") — affirming God's righteousness; even the dreadful bloody judgment is owned as just by the heavenly ministers.

On "because thou hast judged thus," the grammatical-historical note the justification: God is righteous "because thou hast judged thus" — the very judgment (the waters to blood) is the ground of the praise; the retribution (explained in v.6) is just; the angel vindicates God's justice in the plague, far from questioning it. The verse displays the angel of the waters vindicating God's righteous judgment.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the vindication — "the angel of the waters say, Thou art righteous, O Lord... because thou hast judged thus," the angel of the waters declaring God righteous in the judgment (Barnes, Robertson); "which art, and wast, and shalt be," the eternal God justified (1:8, Barnes, Ellicott); "because thou hast judged thus," the very judgment owned as just (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vindicated judgment. The verse marks the angel: of the waters. The verse displays the praise: thou art righteous. The verse displays the God: eternal. The verse displays the ground: thou hast judged thus. The vindication displays God's justice owned in the plague. The verse displays the angel: speaking. The verse displays the righteousness: affirmed. The verse displays the judgment: justified. The angel of the waters vindicating God's righteous judgment displays heaven's response to the bloody plague: the angel of the waters declares God righteous — the eternal God who is, was, and shall be — because he has judged thus; the very judgment owned as just. The verse displays the vindicated judgment, grounded in the blood of the saints (v.6).
6:
"For they have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy." The brief verse notes the retribution of blood for the persecutors of the saints. The grammatical voices note the just recompense. Barnes and Robertson note the ground of the judgment: "they have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy" — because the persecutors shed the blood of the saints and prophets, God has given them blood to drink (the waters turned to blood); the punishment fits the crime — those who shed blood are given blood; the lex talionis of divine justice.

On "they have shed the blood of saints and prophets," the witnesses note the crime. Barnes and Ellicott note that the persecutors "have shed the blood of saints and prophets" — they killed God's faithful people and messengers; the bloodshed of the martyrs is the ground of the bloody retribution; the cry of the martyrs (6:10) is answered.

On "thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy," the grammatical-historical note the fitting penalty: "thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy (deserving)" — the retribution is exactly fitting (blood for bloodshed) and fully deserved; the persecutors are "worthy" of this judgment; God's justice repays the shedders of blood with blood. The verse displays the retribution of blood for the persecutors of the saints.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the ground — "they have shed the blood of saints and prophets, and thou hast given them blood to drink," the bloodshed of the martyrs answered with the judgment of blood (6:10, Barnes, Robertson); "the blood of saints and prophets" the crime of killing God's faithful (Barnes, Ellicott); "thou hast given them blood to drink; for they are worthy," the fitting, deserved retribution, blood for blood (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the just retribution. The verse marks the crime: blood of saints. The verse displays the penalty: blood to drink. The verse displays the fit: blood for blood. The verse displays the desert: they are worthy. The retribution displays the persecutors repaid in kind. The verse displays the blood: shed. The verse displays the drinking: of blood. The verse displays the worthiness: deserved. The retribution of blood for the persecutors of the saints displays the justice of the third plague: because they shed the blood of saints and prophets, God has given them blood to drink — the fitting, deserved retribution, blood for blood, the martyrs' cry answered. The verse displays the just retribution, confirmed from the altar (v.7).
7:
"And I heard another out of the altar say, Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments." The brief verse notes the altar's confirmation of God's true judgments. The grammatical voices note the responding voice. Barnes and Robertson note the confirmation: "I heard another out of the altar say, Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments" — a voice from the altar (perhaps the altar under which the martyrs' souls cried, 6:9-10) responds and confirms the angel's vindication (vv.5-6); "Even so" (yea, amen) affirms that God's judgments are true and righteous.

On "another out of the altar," the witnesses note the source. Barnes and Ellicott note that the voice comes "out of the altar" — perhaps the voice of the martyrs (6:9-10) who had cried "how long?", now confirming that their blood is avenged; the altar of the martyrs answers, vindicating God's judgment.

On "Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments," the grammatical-historical note the doxology of justice: "Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments" — the altar-voice affirms that God's judgments are true (faithful, according to truth) and righteous (just); even the severest plagues are owned as true and righteous; heaven repeatedly vindicates God's justice in the vials (cf. 15:3). The verse displays the altar's confirmation of God's true judgments.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the confirmation — "another out of the altar say, Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments," the altar-voice confirming God's just judgment (Barnes, Robertson); "out of the altar" perhaps the martyrs (6:9-10) whose blood is avenged (Barnes, Ellicott); "true and righteous are thy judgments," the doxology vindicating God's justice (15:3, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the confirmed judgment. The verse marks the voice: from the altar. The verse displays the affirmation: even so. The verse displays the judgments: true and righteous. The verse displays the God: Almighty. The confirmation displays heaven vindicating God's just judgments. The verse displays the altar: speaking. The verse displays the judgments: righteous. The verse displays the amen: even so. The altar's confirmation of God's true judgments displays the second vindication: a voice from the altar answers, "Even so, Lord God Almighty, true and righteous are thy judgments" — the martyrs' altar confirming that God's judgments are true and just. The verse displays the confirmed judgment, before the fourth vial of scorching heat (v.8).
8:
"And the fourth angel poured out his vial upon the sun; and power was given unto him to scorch men with fire." The brief verse notes the fourth vial scorching men with the sun's heat. The grammatical voices note the plague on the sun. Barnes and Robertson note the outpouring: "the fourth angel poured out his vial upon the sun; and power was given unto him to scorch men with fire" — the fourth angel pours his bowl on the sun, which is given power to scorch men with fire; the sun, instead of giving benign light, becomes an instrument of burning judgment; the plague of intense, scorching heat.

On "poured out his vial upon the sun," the witnesses note the affected luminary. Barnes and Henry note that the vial on "the sun" turns the source of light and life into an agent of torment; whether literal scorching heat or (symbolically) a judgment turning what should bless into what afflicts; the fourth plague strikes through the sun.

On "power was given unto him to scorch men with fire," the grammatical-historical note the burning judgment: "power was given unto him to scorch men with fire" — the sun is permitted to burn men with fierce heat; "was given" marks the divine permission; the scorching is a tormenting judgment on the impenitent; yet (v.9) it leads not to repentance but to blasphemy. The verse displays the fourth vial scorching men with the sun's heat.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the plague — "the fourth angel poured out his vial upon the sun; and power was given unto him to scorch men with fire," the sun made to scorch men (Barnes, Robertson); the vial turning the source of light into an agent of torment, literal or symbolic (Barnes, Henry); "power was given unto him to scorch men with fire," the burning judgment by divine permission (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the scorching plague. The verse marks the vial: on the sun. The verse displays the power: to scorch. The verse displays the means: fire. The verse displays the permission: given. The plague displays men scorched by the sun's fierce heat. The verse displays the sun: smitten. The verse displays the scorching: of men. The verse displays the fire: burning. The fourth vial scorching men with the sun's heat displays the fourth plague: the fourth angel pours his bowl on the sun, which is given power to scorch men with fire — the source of light turned to an instrument of burning judgment on the impenitent. The verse displays the scorching plague, met with blasphemy not repentance (v.9).
9:
"And men were scorched with great heat, and blasphemed the name of God, which hath power over these plagues: and they repented not to give him glory." The brief verse notes men blaspheming rather than repenting under the scorching. The grammatical voices note the impenitence. Barnes and Robertson note the hardened response: "men were scorched with great heat, and blasphemed the name of God, which hath power over these plagues: and they repented not to give him glory" — scorched by the great heat, men blasphemed God (who controls the plagues) instead of repenting; the judgment hardened rather than softened them; they did not give God glory.

On "blasphemed the name of God, which hath power over these plagues," the witnesses note the blasphemy. Barnes and Gill note that men "blasphemed the name of God, which hath power over these plagues" — they reviled God, acknowledging (even in their blasphemy) that he controls the plagues, yet hating rather than submitting to him; the impenitent rail against the God who judges them.

On "they repented not to give him glory," the grammatical-historical note the refusal: "they repented not to give him glory" — the plague did not bring repentance; they refused to turn and glorify God; the hardening of the impenitent under judgment (as 9:20-21); judgment alone, without grace, does not soften the rebellious heart but provokes blasphemy. The verse displays men blaspheming rather than repenting under the scorching.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the impenitence — "men were scorched with great heat, and blasphemed the name of God... and they repented not," the scorched men blaspheming rather than repenting (Barnes, Robertson); "blasphemed the name of God, which hath power over these plagues," reviling the God who controls the plagues (Barnes, Gill); "they repented not to give him glory," the hardening of the impenitent (9:20-21, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hardened blasphemers. The verse marks the scorching: great heat. The verse displays the response: blasphemy. The verse displays the acknowledgment: God's power. The verse displays the refusal: repented not. The response displays men hardened and blaspheming under judgment. The verse displays the heat: scorching. The verse displays the blasphemy: uttered. The verse displays the repentance: refused. Men blaspheming rather than repenting under the scorching display the hardness of the impenitent: scorched with great heat, they blasphemed the God who has power over the plagues and repented not to give him glory — judgment hardening rather than softening the rebellious. The verse displays the blaspheming world, before the fifth vial of darkness (v.10).
10:
"And the fifth angel poured out his vial upon the seat of the beast; and his kingdom was full of darkness; and they gnawed their tongues for pain," The witnesses note the fifth vial darkening the beast's kingdom. On "the fifth angel poured out his vial upon the seat of the beast; and his kingdom was full of darkness," the grammatical voices note the plague on the beast's throne. Barnes and Robertson note the outpouring: "the fifth angel poured out his vial upon the seat (throne) of the beast; and his kingdom was full of darkness" — the fifth angel pours his bowl directly on the throne of the beast, and the beast's kingdom is plunged into darkness; the plague strikes at the very center of the antichristian power; recalling the Egyptian darkness (Exodus 10:21-23).

On "upon the seat of the beast... his kingdom was full of darkness," the witnesses note the targeted judgment. Barnes and Ellicott note that the vial on "the seat (throne) of the beast" strikes the seat of his power, and "his kingdom was full of darkness" — the darkness (literal or symbolic of confusion, calamity, and the eclipse of the beast's power) overspreads his realm; in the historicist reading, the darkening and weakening of the antichristian (papal) power.

On "they gnawed their tongues for pain," the grammatical-historical note the anguish: "they gnawed their tongues for pain (anguish)" — the subjects of the beast's kingdom gnaw their tongues in agony; the figure of intense pain and distress under the plague; the darkness brings torment as well as confusion. The verse displays the fifth vial darkening the beast's kingdom.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the plague — "the fifth angel poured out his vial upon the seat of the beast; and his kingdom was full of darkness," the bowl on the beast's throne darkening his kingdom (Exodus 10:21, Barnes, Robertson); "the seat of the beast" the center of his power, the darkness eclipsing the antichristian power to historicists (Barnes, Ellicott); "they gnawed their tongues for pain," the anguish of his subjects (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the darkened kingdom. The verse marks the vial: on the beast's throne. The verse displays the kingdom: full of darkness. The verse displays the pain: gnawed tongues. The verse displays the parallel: Egypt's darkness. The plague displays the beast's realm darkened and in anguish. The verse displays the throne: smitten. The verse displays the darkness: spread. The verse displays the pain: gnawing. The fifth vial darkening the beast's kingdom displays the fifth plague: the fifth angel pours his bowl on the throne of the beast, and his kingdom is filled with darkness, and men gnaw their tongues for pain — the judgment striking the center of the antichristian power, recalling Egypt's darkness. The verse displays the darkened kingdom, met again with blasphemy (v.11).
11:
"And blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pains and their sores, and repented not of their deeds." The brief verse notes the renewed blasphemy and impenitence under the fifth plague. The grammatical voices note the hardened response. Barnes and Robertson note the repeated impenitence: "blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pains and their sores, and repented not of their deeds" — under the fifth plague, men again blaspheme the God of heaven because of their pains (the darkness, v.10) and their sores (the first plague, v.2, still afflicting them), and again do not repent; the hardening deepens.

On "blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pains and their sores," the witnesses note the blasphemy. Barnes and Gill note that men "blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pains and their sores" — they revile God for their sufferings; the accumulating plagues (pains and sores) provoke not repentance but blasphemy; they curse God for the very judgments their sins have brought.

On "repented not of their deeds," the grammatical-historical note the persistent impenitence: "repented not of their deeds" — they would not repent of their evil deeds; the refrain (vv.9, 11) stresses the obstinate hardness of the impenitent; the plagues, however severe, do not bring the rebellious to repentance; the heart unchanged by grace only hardens under wrath. The verse displays the renewed blasphemy and impenitence under the fifth plague.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the impenitence — "blasphemed the God of heaven because of their pains and their sores, and repented not of their deeds," the renewed blasphemy and refusal to repent under the plague (Barnes, Robertson); "because of their pains and their sores," reviling God for their accumulating sufferings (v.2, v.10, Barnes, Gill); "repented not of their deeds," the obstinate hardness of the impenitent (vv.9, 11, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the hardened blasphemers. The verse marks the blasphemy: of the God of heaven. The verse displays the cause: pains and sores. The verse displays the refusal: repented not. The verse displays the deeds: unrepented. The response displays men hardened and blaspheming under accumulating plagues. The verse displays the blasphemy: renewed. The verse displays the pains: cursed. The verse displays the repentance: refused. The renewed blasphemy and impenitence under the fifth plague display the deepening hardness: men blasphemed the God of heaven for their pains and sores and repented not of their deeds — the accumulating judgments provoking blasphemy, not repentance. The verse displays the impenitent world, before the sixth vial on the Euphrates (v.12).
12:
"And the sixth angel poured out his vial upon the great river Euphrates; and the water thereof was dried up, that the way of the kings of the east might be prepared." The witnesses note the sixth vial drying the Euphrates for the kings of the east. On "the sixth angel poured out his vial upon the great river Euphrates; and the water thereof was dried up," the grammatical voices note the dried river. Barnes and Robertson note the outpouring: "the sixth angel poured out his vial upon the great river Euphrates; and the water thereof was dried up" — the sixth angel pours his bowl on the Euphrates, which dries up; the drying of the great river removes a barrier (as the drying of the Red Sea and Jordan); in the historicist reading, the drying of the Euphrates is read of the decline and removal of the Turkish/Ottoman power (the obstacle to the gospel in the East).

On "that the way of the kings of the east might be prepared," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Ellicott note that the river was dried "that the way of the kings of the east might be prepared" — the drying opens a way for "the kings of the east" (kings from the rising of the sun); interpreters vary — the historicists read it of the way prepared for the conversion of the eastern nations (or the Jews) to the gospel, or for the powers that overthrow the antichristian Babylon; the futurist reads literal kings from the east advancing for the final battle (vv.14, 16).

On the kings of the east, the puritan Henry notes the providential opening: the drying of the river prepares the way for God's purposes — whether judgment on Babylon or the ingathering of the nations; God removes the obstacles to the advance of his appointed agents. The verse displays the sixth vial drying the Euphrates for the kings of the east.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the dried river — "the sixth angel poured out his vial upon the great river Euphrates; and the water thereof was dried up," the Euphrates dried, a barrier removed, the Ottoman power to historicists (Barnes, Robertson); "that the way of the kings of the east might be prepared," the way opened for the kings of the east — the eastern nations' conversion or the agents against Babylon (historicist), or literal kings for the final battle (futurist, Barnes, Ellicott); the providential opening for God's purposes (Henry). The verse displays the dried Euphrates. The verse marks the vial: on the Euphrates. The verse displays the drying: of the river. The verse displays the purpose: the kings of the east. The verse displays the way: prepared. The plague displays the barrier removed for the appointed advance. The verse displays the river: dried. The verse displays the kings: of the east. The verse displays the way: opened. The sixth vial drying the Euphrates for the kings of the east displays the sixth plague: the sixth angel pours his bowl on the Euphrates, which dries up to prepare the way of the kings of the east — read by historicists of the removal of the Ottoman barrier and the opening for the nations, by futurists of literal kings advancing to the final battle. The verse displays the dried river, before the gathering of the kings (vv.13-16).
13:
"And I saw three unclean spirits like frogs come out of the mouth of the dragon, and out of the mouth of the beast, and out of the mouth of the false prophet." The witnesses note the three frog-like unclean spirits from the unholy trinity. On "three unclean spirits like frogs come out of the mouth of the dragon, and... the beast, and... the false prophet," the grammatical voices note the demonic spirits. Barnes and Robertson note the vision: "three unclean spirits like frogs come out of the mouth of the dragon, and out of the mouth of the beast, and out of the mouth of the false prophet" — three unclean spirits, like frogs, issue from the mouths of the dragon (Satan), the beast (the persecuting power), and the false prophet (the second beast); the unholy trinity sends forth its lying, seducing spirits; "like frogs" recalls the Egyptian plague of frogs (Exodus 8) and marks them as loathsome, unclean.

On "out of the mouth of the dragon, and... the beast, and... the false prophet," the witnesses note the source. Barnes and Gill note that the spirits come from the "mouth" of the three — from their lying speech, propaganda, and deceiving influence; the dragon, beast, and false prophet together send forth the spirits that deceive the nations (v.14); the three forms of the antichristian power united in deception.

On "unclean spirits like frogs," the grammatical-historical note the loathsome deceivers: the spirits "like frogs" are unclean, croaking, loathsome — the figure of foul, deceiving influences; whether demonic spirits or the lying doctrines and propaganda that go forth from the antichristian powers to gather the world to battle. The verse displays the three frog-like unclean spirits from the unholy trinity.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the spirits — "three unclean spirits like frogs come out of the mouth of the dragon, and... the beast, and... the false prophet," the demonic deceiving spirits from the unholy trinity (Barnes, Robertson); "out of the mouth" the lying speech and deceiving influence of the three (Barnes, Gill); "like frogs" the loathsome, unclean deceivers (Exodus 8, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the frog-like spirits. The verse marks the spirits: three, unclean. The verse displays the source: the unholy trinity's mouths. The verse displays the figure: frogs. The verse displays the influence: deceiving. The vision displays the deceiving spirits sent from the antichristian powers. The verse displays the spirits: issuing. The verse displays the dragon, beast, prophet: their mouths. The verse displays the frogs: unclean. The three frog-like unclean spirits from the unholy trinity display the demonic deception before the final battle: three unclean spirits like frogs come from the mouths of the dragon, the beast, and the false prophet — the lying, seducing spirits of the antichristian powers, loathsome as Egypt's frogs. The verse displays the deceiving spirits, gathering the kings to battle (v.14).
14:
"For they are the spirits of devils, working miracles, which go forth unto the kings of the earth and of the whole world, to gather them to the battle of that great day of God Almighty." The witnesses note the demonic spirits gathering the kings to the final battle. On "they are the spirits of devils, working miracles," the grammatical voices note their nature. Barnes and Robertson note the identification: "they are the spirits of devils (demons), working miracles (signs)" — the frog-like spirits (v.13) are demonic spirits that work signs to deceive; the lying wonders (as 13:13-14; 2 Thessalonians 2:9) by which they seduce the kings of the earth.

On "which go forth unto the kings of the earth and of the whole world, to gather them to the battle," the witnesses note the gathering. Barnes and Ellicott note that the spirits "go forth unto the kings of the earth and of the whole world, to gather them to the battle" — they deceive and assemble the kings of the whole world for the great final conflict against God and his people; the demonic deception unites the world's powers for the last battle.

On "the battle of that great day of God Almighty," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the great day: the gathering is "to the battle of that great day of God Almighty" — the climactic conflict on the great day of God's judgment; the assembling of the antichristian powers for the final battle (Armageddon, v.16), which ends in their overthrow; the great day when God Almighty triumphs over his gathered enemies. The verse displays the demonic spirits gathering the kings to the final battle.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the spirits — "they are the spirits of devils, working miracles," the demonic spirits working lying signs to deceive (13:13; 2 Thessalonians 2:9, Barnes, Robertson); "go forth unto the kings of the earth... to gather them to the battle," assembling the world's kings for the final conflict (Barnes, Ellicott); "the battle of that great day of God Almighty," the climactic battle ending in God's triumph (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the gathering to battle. The verse marks the spirits: of devils. The verse displays the signs: working miracles. The verse displays the gathering: the kings of the world. The verse displays the battle: the great day. The spirits display the world's powers assembled for the final conflict. The verse displays the spirits: demonic. The verse displays the kings: gathered. The verse displays the day: great. The demonic spirits gathering the kings to the final battle display the purpose of the deceiving spirits: they are the spirits of devils, working signs, going forth to gather the kings of the whole world to the battle of the great day of God Almighty — the antichristian powers assembled for the final conflict that ends in God's triumph. The verse displays the gathering, interrupted by Christ's warning (v.15).
15:
"Behold, I come as a thief. Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments, lest he walk naked, and they see his shame." The witnesses note Christ's interjected call to watchfulness. On "Behold, I come as a thief," the grammatical voices note the sudden coming. Barnes and Robertson note the interjection of Christ's voice: "Behold, I come as a thief" — amid the gathering for battle, Christ suddenly speaks, warning that he comes unexpectedly, as a thief in the night (Matthew 24:43; 1 Thessalonians 5:2; Revelation 3:3); the suddenness of his coming calls for constant readiness.

On "Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments," the witnesses note the beatitude. Barnes and Ellicott note the blessing: "Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments" — blessed is the one who stays awake (spiritually vigilant) and keeps his garments (his readiness, purity, righteousness, like one dressed and prepared); the watchful, ready believer is blessed at Christ's coming.

On "lest he walk naked, and they see his shame," the grammatical-historical note the warning: the unwatchful is in danger "lest he walk naked, and they see his shame" — the figure of one caught unprepared, found naked (stripped of righteousness/readiness) and exposed to shame; the warning to be ready, clothed in righteousness, lest Christ's sudden coming find one shamefully unprepared. The verse displays Christ's interjected call to watchfulness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the coming — "Behold, I come as a thief," Christ's sudden, unexpected coming, calling for readiness (Matthew 24:43; 1 Thessalonians 5:2, Barnes, Robertson); "Blessed is he that watcheth, and keepeth his garments," the blessing on the vigilant, ready believer (Barnes, Ellicott); "lest he walk naked, and they see his shame," the warning against being caught unprepared and exposed (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the call to watch. The verse marks the coming: as a thief. The verse displays the blessing: he that watcheth. The verse displays the garments: kept. The verse displays the warning: lest shame. The interjection displays Christ calling for constant readiness. The verse displays the coming: sudden. The verse displays the watching: blessed. The verse displays the nakedness: shameful. Christ's interjected call to watchfulness displays the sudden word amid the gathering: "Behold, I come as a thief; blessed is he that watcheth and keeps his garments, lest he walk naked and they see his shame" — Christ's coming is sudden, and the watchful, ready believer is blessed. The verse displays the call to watch, before the gathering at Armageddon (v.16).
16:
"And he gathered them together into a place called in the Hebrew tongue Armageddon." The witnesses note the gathering of the kings at Armageddon — the crux of the name. On "he gathered them together into a place," the grammatical voices note the assembling. Barnes and Robertson note that the demonic spirits (vv.13-14) "gathered them together (the kings of the earth) into a place" — the world's powers are assembled in one place for the great final battle; the gathering is the climax of the demonic deception.

On "a place called in the Hebrew tongue Armageddon," interpreters note the meaning of the name. Barnes and Robertson note that "Armageddon" (Greek Harmagedon) is from the Hebrew "Har Megiddo" — the "Mountain (or hill) of Megiddo"; Megiddo, in the plain of Esdraelon/Jezreel, was a famous battlefield (Judges 5:19, Barak and Sisera; 2 Chronicles 35:22; 2 Kings 23:29-30, where Josiah was slain; Zechariah 12:11, the mourning of Hadadrimmon in the valley of Megiddon). The name marks a place of decisive battle and great slaughter.

On the significance, the grammatical-historical note the symbolic battlefield: "Armageddon" is taken as the symbolic name for the place of the final, decisive conflict between the powers of evil and God; whether a literal location or (more commonly) a symbol drawn from the historic battlefield of Megiddo, signifying the great climactic battle where the antichristian powers are gathered and overthrown; the name evokes decisive divine victory. The verse displays the gathering of the kings at Armageddon.

The witnesses agree the substance: the demonic spirits gather the kings of the earth into one place, "Armageddon" — the Mountain of Megiddo, the historic battlefield (Judges 5:19; 2 Kings 23:29; Zechariah 12:11), here the symbolic place of the great final conflict (Barnes, Robertson). The chief question is the name's meaning and reference: literally Har-Megiddo, and symbolically the place of the decisive last battle. The verse displays the Armageddon crux. The verse marks the gathering: into one place. The verse displays the name: Armageddon. The verse displays the meaning: Mountain of Megiddo. The verse displays the significance: the final battle. The gathering displays the world's powers assembled at the symbolic battlefield. The verse displays the kings: gathered. The verse displays the place: Armageddon. The verse displays the battle: final. The gathering of the kings at Armageddon displays the assembling for the last conflict: the spirits gather the kings into a place called in Hebrew Armageddon — the Mountain of Megiddo, the historic battlefield, here the symbolic place of the great final battle in which the antichristian powers are gathered and overthrown. The verse displays Armageddon, before the seventh and last vial (v.17).
17:
"And the seventh angel poured out his vial into the air; and there came a great voice out of the temple of heaven, from the throne, saying, It is done." The witnesses note the seventh vial and the great voice declaring "It is done." On "the seventh angel poured out his vial into the air," the grammatical voices note the final plague. Barnes and Robertson note the outpouring: "the seventh angel poured out his vial into the air" — the seventh and last angel pours his bowl into the air; the realm of the powers of the air (Ephesians 2:2), or the all-encompassing element; the final plague, completing the wrath (15:1), is poured out.

On "there came a great voice out of the temple of heaven, from the throne, saying, It is done," the witnesses note the declaration. Barnes and Ellicott note the announcement: "a great voice out of the temple of heaven, from the throne, saying, It is done" — a great voice from the throne in the heavenly temple declares "It is done (it is finished, gegonen)"; the outpouring of God's wrath is completed; the consummation of the judgments is announced; the end has come.

On "It is done," the puritan Henry notes the finality: "It is done" — the word of completion; the wrath of God is filled up (15:1); the judgments are finished; the declaration from the throne marks the consummation of God's purpose against the antichristian world; what God appointed is now accomplished. The verse displays the seventh vial and the great voice declaring "It is done."

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the final plague — "the seventh angel poured out his vial into the air," the last bowl poured into the air, completing the wrath (15:1, Barnes, Robertson); "a great voice out of the temple of heaven, from the throne, saying, It is done," the throne declaring the judgments completed (Barnes, Ellicott); "It is done," the word of finality, the consummation of God's purpose (15:1, Henry). The verse displays the completed wrath. The verse marks the vial: into the air. The verse displays the voice: from the throne. The verse displays the declaration: it is done. The verse displays the wrath: filled up. The plague displays the judgments brought to their consummation. The verse displays the vial: poured. The verse displays the voice: great. The verse displays the end: come. The seventh vial and the great voice declaring "It is done" display the consummation of the wrath: the seventh angel pours his bowl into the air, and a great voice from the throne declares "It is done" — the judgments completed, God's wrath filled up. The verse displays the finished wrath, with the mighty earthquake and the fall of Babylon (vv.18-21).
18:
"And there were voices, and thunders, and lightnings; and there was a great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth, so mighty an earthquake, and so great." The brief verse notes the unprecedented earthquake at the seventh vial. The grammatical voices note the convulsions. Barnes and Robertson note the tokens of judgment: "there were voices, and thunders, and lightnings; and there was a great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth, so mighty an earthquake, and so great" — the seventh vial is accompanied by voices, thunders, lightnings, and a great earthquake — the greatest ever known; the convulsions of nature mark the consummating judgment.

On "voices, and thunders, and lightnings," the witnesses note the theophany. Barnes and Ellicott note that the "voices, thunders, and lightnings" (as 4:5; 8:5; 11:19) are the tokens of God's majestic, awful presence in judgment; the storm of divine wrath attends the final plague.

On "a great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth," the eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the unparalleled convulsion: the earthquake was "such as was not since men were upon the earth, so mighty... and so great" — an unprecedented, supreme convulsion; the figure of a vast, final overthrow and upheaval, shaking the whole order of the antichristian world; the greatest of all judgments, beyond any before it. The verse displays the unprecedented earthquake at the seventh vial.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the convulsions — "voices, and thunders, and lightnings; and there was a great earthquake, such as was not since men were upon the earth," the tokens of judgment and the greatest-ever earthquake (Barnes, Robertson); "voices, thunders, lightnings" the tokens of God's awful presence (4:5; 11:19, Barnes, Ellicott); "such as was not since men were upon the earth," the unparalleled, supreme convulsion (Cyril, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the great earthquake. The verse marks the tokens: voices, thunders, lightnings. The verse displays the earthquake: unprecedented. The verse displays the magnitude: mighty and great. The verse displays the judgment: consummating. The convulsions display the supreme upheaval of the final judgment. The verse displays the thunders: sounding. The verse displays the earthquake: greatest. The verse displays the upheaval: vast. The unprecedented earthquake at the seventh vial displays the convulsions of the consummating judgment: voices, thunders, lightnings, and a great earthquake — the greatest since men were on the earth — attend the seventh vial; the supreme upheaval shaking the whole order of the antichristian world. The verse displays the great earthquake, dividing Babylon (v.19).
19:
"And the great city was divided into three parts, and the cities of the nations fell: and great Babylon came in remembrance before God, to give unto her the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath." The witnesses note the fall of Babylon and the cities under the seventh vial. On "the great city was divided into three parts, and the cities of the nations fell," the grammatical voices note the collapse. Barnes and Robertson note the overthrow: "the great city was divided into three parts, and the cities of the nations fell" — the great city (Babylon) is split into three parts (broken, disintegrated), and the cities of the nations fall; the earthquake (v.18) topples the antichristian city and the cities of the world; the collapse of the structures of the antichristian order.

On "great Babylon came in remembrance before God," the witnesses note the judgment recalled. Barnes and Ellicott note that "great Babylon came in remembrance before God" — God remembers Babylon (her sins, 18:5), bringing her to the long-deserved judgment; the time of her reckoning has come; God's remembrance of her crimes issues in her punishment.

On "to give unto her the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath," the grammatical-historical note the cup of wrath: God gives Babylon "the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath" — the cup of his fierce anger (Jeremiah 25:15; Revelation 14:10); she who made the nations drink her wine of fornication (14:8) now drinks the cup of God's wrath; the fitting retribution on the great corrupt city. The verse displays the fall of Babylon and the cities under the seventh vial.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the collapse — "the great city was divided into three parts, and the cities of the nations fell," the antichristian city and the world's cities toppled by the earthquake (Barnes, Robertson); "great Babylon came in remembrance before God," God remembering Babylon's sins for judgment (18:5, Barnes, Ellicott); "the cup of the wine of the fierceness of his wrath," the cup of God's fierce anger, fitting retribution (Jeremiah 25:15; 14:8, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the fall of Babylon. The verse marks the city: divided. The verse displays the nations' cities: fallen. The verse displays the remembrance: of Babylon. The verse displays the cup: of wrath. The judgment displays Babylon brought to her reckoning. The verse displays the city: broken. The verse displays the Babylon: remembered. The verse displays the cup: given. The fall of Babylon and the cities under the seventh vial display the consummating judgment: the great city is split in three and the cities of the nations fall, and great Babylon comes in remembrance before God to receive the cup of the wine of his fierce wrath — the corrupt city brought to her long-deserved reckoning. The verse displays the fallen Babylon, amid fleeing islands and falling hail (vv.20-21).
20:
"And every island fled away, and the mountains were not found." The very brief verse notes the islands and mountains vanishing. The grammatical voices note the cosmic upheaval. Barnes and Robertson note the figure: "every island fled away, and the mountains were not found" — under the great earthquake (v.18), every island flees and the mountains disappear; the figure of a total, sweeping upheaval; the most stable and permanent features of the earth are removed; the convulsion overturns the whole established order.

On "every island fled away," the witnesses note the disappearance. Barnes and Ellicott note that "every island fled away" — the islands vanish, as if fleeing; the figure (drawn from 6:14) of the dissolution of the existing order; whether literal or symbolic of the overthrow of nations and kingdoms.

On "the mountains were not found," the grammatical-historical note the vanished mountains: "the mountains were not found" — the mountains, the symbols of stability and permanence, disappear; the figure of the complete overthrow of the established powers and structures; nothing of the old order stands; the consummating judgment levels all. The verse displays the islands and mountains vanishing.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the upheaval — "every island fled away, and the mountains were not found," the islands and mountains vanishing under the great earthquake, a sweeping upheaval (6:14, Barnes, Robertson); "every island fled away" the dissolution of the existing order, literal or symbolic (Barnes, Ellicott); "the mountains were not found," the symbols of permanence removed, the established powers overthrown (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the vanishing world. The verse marks the islands: fled. The verse displays the mountains: not found. The verse displays the upheaval: total. The verse displays the order: overthrown. The convulsion displays the whole established order swept away. The verse displays the islands: gone. The verse displays the mountains: vanished. The verse displays the world: shaken. The islands and mountains vanishing display the sweeping upheaval of the final judgment: every island flees and the mountains are not found — the figure of a total convulsion overthrowing the whole established order, nothing of the old standing. The verse displays the vanished world, struck by the great hail (v.21).
21:
"And there fell upon men a great hail out of heaven, every stone about the weight of a talent: and men blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail; for it was exceeding great." The witnesses note the great hail and the final blasphemy of the impenitent. On "there fell upon men a great hail out of heaven, every stone about the weight of a talent," the grammatical voices note the plague of hail. Barnes and Robertson note the outpouring: "there fell upon men a great hail out of heaven, every stone about the weight of a talent" — a great hail falls, each stone about a talent in weight (very heavy, 50-100 pounds); the plague of enormous, crushing hailstones (recalling the Egyptian hail, Exodus 9:23-24, and the seventh trumpet, 11:19); the final, devastating judgment.

On "every stone about the weight of a talent," the witnesses note the crushing weight. Barnes and Ellicott note that each hailstone "about the weight of a talent" was enormous and destructive; the figure of a terrible, crushing judgment; whether literal hail or symbolic of a devastating outpouring of wrath.

On "men blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail; for it was exceeding great," the grammatical-historical note the final impenitence: "men blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail; for it was exceeding great" — even under this last, dreadful plague, men blaspheme God rather than repent; the impenitent, to the very end, harden themselves and curse God; the refrain of blasphemy (vv.9, 11) closes the vials; judgment alone, to the last, does not soften the rebellious. The verse displays the great hail and the final blasphemy of the impenitent.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the hail — "there fell upon men a great hail out of heaven, every stone about the weight of a talent," the crushing hailstones of the final plague (Exodus 9:23; 11:19, Barnes, Robertson); "every stone about the weight of a talent" the enormous, destructive weight (Barnes, Ellicott); "men blasphemed God because of the plague of the hail," the final impenitent blasphemy, to the very end (vv.9, 11, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the last plague. The verse marks the hail: great. The verse displays the stones: a talent each. The verse displays the response: blasphemy. The verse displays the impenitence: to the end. The plague displays men crushed yet blaspheming under the final judgment. The verse displays the hail: falling. The verse displays the weight: crushing. The verse displays the blasphemy: final. The great hail and the final blasphemy of the impenitent display the close of the vials: a great hail, each stone a talent's weight, falls on men, who blaspheme God for the exceeding great plague rather than repent — the impenitent hardened to the very end. The verse displays the finished vials, before the judgment of the great whore (ch.17).
